topic: Missing arguments — cross-cutting insights from Phase 1 that aren't captured anywhere
our_argument_summary: The review currently structures its argument as: (1) model description, (2) point-by-point WIN review, (3) predictions page analysis, (4) falsification tests + internal contradictions + code analysis, (5) AI directive analysis, (6) conclusions with 12 logical patterns. Phase 1 curmudgeon review of all 67 WINs, 30+ sections, and 8 holistic checks has surfaced several cross-cutting arguments that are not made anywhere in the current review text.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #1: The 'Quadruple Failure' pattern. Phase 1 code_analysis reveals that 34 of 64 reviewed WINs simultaneously exhibit ALL FOUR structural failures: (a) hardcoded or absent monitoring, (b) relabels standard physics, (c) post-hoc retrodiction, (d) does NOT derive from dome geometry. This is a devastating statistic that the review never presents as a unified argument. Part 4.6 discusses each pattern individually (monitoring illusion, relabeling, post-hoc) but never cross-tabulates them. A dome defender can claim each individual pattern is debatable, but the INTERSECTION of all four is much harder to dismiss. 34/64 = 53% of reviewed WINs fail every structural test simultaneously.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a subsection to Part 4.6 or Part 6 (Conclusions) presenting the cross-tabulation: 'Of 64 WINs with completed code analysis, 34 (53%) simultaneously exhibit all four structural failures — their monitoring is absent or hardcoded, they relabel existing standard physics, they adopt known observations as post-hoc predictions, and none of their claimed values derive from dome geometry. These are not predictions in any scientific sense.' List the 34 WINs explicitly.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #2: The 'Axial Symmetry Paradox.' The dome's core field equation H(r) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) depends only on radial distance r from the disc center and is therefore perfectly axially symmetric. Yet at least 12 WINs (004, 005, 024, 028, 035, 036, 040, 041, 043, 059, 060, 063) claim the model explains longitude-dependent phenomena: the SAA's two-cell structure, its drift westward, the North Magnetic Pole's acceleration toward Siberia, hemispheric asymmetry in decay rates, the Bermuda-Japan 'symmetry.' An axially symmetric model CANNOT produce longitude-dependent features without additional free parameters — which the dome does not disclose. This is a structural impossibility that appears in scattered WIN reviews (WIN-040, 041, 043, 059, 063) but is never stated as a unified argument anywhere in the review.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a new internal contradiction to Part 4.5 or a new subsection under Part 4.6: 'The dome's field equation is radially symmetric (it depends only on distance from the disc center). Any phenomenon that varies with longitude — SAA cell positions, NMP trajectory, hemispheric asymmetry — is structurally impossible for this equation to predict. The dome claims 12+ such phenomena as confirmed predictions.' This is arguably a new kill-shot: the model's own mathematics provably cannot generate the features it claims to predict.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #3: The 'Batch Registration' pattern. Phase 1 reviews of WIN-035 through WIN-042 revealed that these 8 WINs were all registered on the same date (2026-03-06) and confirmed within 6 days. All use INTERMAGNET data that was publicly available for years before registration. This is not an isolated case — it represents a systematic method of inflating the WIN count by registering known observations as 'predictions' in bulk. The review discusses individual WINs' short prediction-to-confirmation windows but never identifies the batch pattern as systemic. A dome defender confronted with one 6-day prediction can claim urgency; confronted with 8 simultaneous 6-day predictions, the pattern is undeniable.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph to Section 3.5.9 (Structural Pattern: The Predictions Page) or Part 6 (Conclusions) documenting the batch registration dates. Show that clusters of WINs were registered simultaneously from pre-existing data. Include the specific dates and WIN numbers.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #4: The 'Duplicate Counting' cascade. Phase 1 revealed extensive double- and triple-counting that inflates the WIN total far beyond what individual verdict assessments capture. At least three major clusters: (a) Tidal WINs 045/046/049/050/051 — five WINs for one geometric argument (local moon → one tidal spike, not two), (b) Schumann WINs 001/002/029/038/061 — five WINs from one physical system (cavity resonance), (c) SAA/NMP WINs 004/005/006/007/035/036/037/040/041/042/043/059/060/063 — fourteen WINs from INTERMAGNET geomagnetic data, many logically entailed by each other (e.g., WIN-037 'decay >= 28 nT' and WIN-042 'multi-station decay >= 28 nT' are the same threshold applied to the same dataset). The review mentions duplication for individual WINs (especially tidal), and Section 6.1 lists 'Multiple counting' as Pattern #5, but never quantifies the effective WIN count after deduplication. If the 67 WINs reduce to ~25-30 independent claims after deduplication, that's a powerful argument the review should make explicitly.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a subsection to Part 3.5.9 or Part 6 that groups WINs into independent claim clusters and counts: 'After removing duplicate/entailed claims, the dome model's 67 WINs reduce to approximately N independent physical claims.' Even an approximate count (showing that tidal = 1 claim, Schumann = 1 claim, SAA/NMP = ~5 independent claims) would be devastating.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #5: The 'Derives from Dome' devastation. Only 4 of 64 reviewed WINs (WIN-020, WIN-044, WIN-057, WIN-058) have derives_from_dome=true. The review presents this statistic in Part 4.6.3 but never examines WHAT HAPPENS when the dome actually uses its own geometry. Phase 1 reviews found: WIN-020 (gear ratios reproduce 18.6-yr cycle — but gears are a MECHANISM for matching a known value, not a derivation FROM dome parameters), WIN-044 (FSF formula — hidden dependency on globe-derived distances), WIN-057 (two-zone topology — actually regressed accuracy), WIN-058 (unified angular coordinate — just a fitted parameter). In other words, even the 4 'derives' cases are problematic. The dome model has ZERO WINs where dome geometry makes a clean, falsifiable, prospective numerical prediction that was subsequently confirmed. This is the single most devastating finding of the entire Phase 1 review and it appears nowhere as a unified argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a subsection examining the 4 'derives_from_dome' WINs individually. Show that even in these best cases, the 'derivation' is either circular (uses globe inputs), regressive (makes accuracy worse), or just parameter-fitting. Conclude: 'The dome model has not produced a single prediction that is simultaneously (a) derived from dome geometry, (b) stated before the observation, and (c) confirmed by independent measurement.'

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #6: The 'Monitor Doesn't Believe It' pattern. Phase 1 discovered that the dome's own monitoring system (monitor.py) conspicuously omits certain categories of WINs. The theological/unfalsifiable WINs (031, 032, 034) have no monitoring domains. The cosmological WINs (047, 048, 052, 054, 055) have no monitoring domains. The analemma/gyroscope WINs (018, 019, 021) have no monitoring domains. Even the parallax WIN (017) — supposedly a key claim — has no monitoring. If the dome's author built a 39-domain monitoring system but chose NOT to monitor these claims, that's an implicit admission that they can't be tested. This meta-argument appears in scattered WIN reviews but is never unified.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph to Part 4.6.1 or Part 5.16 listing the categories of WINs the monitoring system avoids: cosmological, theological, geometric (parallax, analemma). Note that the system's author chose to monitor what CAN be hardcoded (INTERMAGNET station values) but avoided claims where live data would reveal failures.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #7: The 'Kernels of Truth' synthesis. Phase 1 identified genuine kernels of truth in almost every WIN — real phenomena the dome model correctly identifies (Schumann stability, SAA decay rates, NMP acceleration, tidal constituent periods, the RAR). Our review never synthesizes these into a positive acknowledgment: 'The dome model correctly identifies approximately N real physical phenomena. Its authors demonstrate genuine scientific literacy in data mining observational databases. The failure is not in observation but in attribution — every phenomenon the model identifies is already explained by standard physics, and the dome's own geometry cannot reproduce any of them.' This positive framing was recommended by the HOL-STRESSTEST review but has not been implemented.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph to the Executive Summary or Conclusions that explicitly credits the dome model's observational accuracy while distinguishing observation from prediction. This preempts the dome defender's strongest objection ('you just refuse to acknowledge our data is correct').

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #8: The 'Falsifiability Asymmetry.' The review's Evaluation Guide establishes what would count as a dome success, but never states what would change OUR minds. Phase 1 stress-testing identified this as the single strongest objection a dome defender can make: 'Your review is unfalsifiable — you've defined your categories so nothing CAN count as a dome win.' A concrete 'what would change our verdict' statement would preempt this entirely. Example: 'We would upgrade a WIN to a confirmed prediction if (a) the predicted value was stated before the measurement, (b) the value derives from dome geometry, not globe parameters, (c) standard physics does not independently predict the same value, and (d) the measurement confirms the prediction within stated error bars.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a 'What Would Change Our Assessment' subsection to the Evaluation Guide or Part 6. State the concrete, falsifiable criteria under which a WIN would be accepted as a genuine dome prediction. The HOL-STRESSTEST review already recommended this — implement it.

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #9: The 'Eclipse Pre-commitment Gap.' The dome model has registered specific predictions with error bars for the August 2026 eclipse. Our review discusses the eclipse test framework (Section 3.5.7, 6.2) but never commits to how we will evaluate the results. If the dome's eclipse predictions fall within their stated error bars (even if explicable by Chapman-layer ionospheric physics), we should state NOW what our response will be. Without this pre-commitment, the dome can claim post-hoc that any review response to good eclipse results is goalpost-moving.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a pre-commitment statement to Section 6.2 or a new Section 6.4: 'If the dome model's eclipse magnetic predictions are confirmed within their stated error bars, this review will acknowledge the confirmation and evaluate whether (a) the prediction derives uniquely from dome geometry, (b) standard ionospheric physics independently predicts the same values, and (c) the prediction window was truly prospective.'

holes_found.description: MISSING CROSS-CUTTING ARGUMENT #10: The 'Open Problems Concession.' The dome model's own 'Open Problems' page (OPEN-001, OPEN-003, OPEN-007) concedes it cannot function without WGS84 parameters (standard geodesy). This is mentioned in CLAUDE.md but barely appears in the review text. The dome's own admissions that it relies on globe infrastructure (WGS84 coordinates, INTERMAGNET station positions in lat/lon, NOAA databases that use globe models) deserve a unified treatment.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph to Part 1.4 or Part 6 synthesizing the dome's own conceded dependencies on globe infrastructure. This is strong because it's not our claim — it's the dome's own admission.

stronger_arguments.description: The 'Zero Clean Predictions' argument: After reviewing all 67 WINs, Phase 1 found exactly ZERO that satisfy all four criteria of a genuine scientific prediction: (a) derived from dome geometry, (b) stated prospectively, (c) not predicted by standard physics, (d) confirmed by independent measurement. This is the review's strongest possible conclusion and should be stated explicitly with the supporting tag statistics.
sources: derives_from_dome count = 4, and even those 4 are problematic

stronger_arguments.description: The 'Information Content' argument: Many dome predictions have near-zero information content because they predict the continuation of known trends. Predicting that SAA decay will exceed 28 nT/yr when it's been 25-35 nT/yr for decades, or that the NMP will deviate >18° from 120°E when it's already at 139°E and accelerating, are not scientifically meaningful predictions. The review addresses this per-WIN but never frames it as a systemic pattern.
sources: WIN-035, WIN-036, WIN-037, WIN-038, WIN-040, WIN-042 reviews

stronger_arguments.description: The 'Cosmological Incoherence Cluster': WINs 047, 048, 052, 054, 055 reference phenomena at cosmological scales (redshifts, CMB, galaxy clusters) that exist entirely OUTSIDE the dome's physical framework. The dome has no cosmological model — it describes a local structure (20,015 km radius disc with 9,086 km firmament). These WINs amount to 'ΛCDM has anomalies, therefore dome' — a non sequitur that deserves to be called out as a pattern.
sources: WIN-047, WIN-048, WIN-052, WIN-054, WIN-055 reviews

stronger_arguments.description: Cross-tabulation of code_analysis tags with verdicts. Do certain verdict categories correlate with certain tag patterns? For instance, are all 'Self-Contradicted' WINs also 'derives_from_dome=false'? Are all 'Refuted by Data' WINs also 'hardcoded'? This correlation analysis could reveal that the structural tag patterns predict the verdict outcomes, suggesting the verdict assignment is robust and not arbitrary.

kernel_of_truth.description: To be fair, identifying missing arguments in one's own review is inherently easier than identifying them in someone else's work. The fact that Phase 1 surfaced many cross-cutting themes demonstrates the review pipeline is working — systematic per-item review naturally reveals patterns invisible from the top down.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The missing arguments represent genuine gaps in the review's persuasive completeness. A dome defender who reads the review carefully will notice these unstated patterns and may interpret the omission as uncertainty or oversight on our part.

deeper_analysis:
Phase 1 reviewed all 67 WINs, 30+ sections, and 8 prior holistic checks. The most striking finding is that several of the review's most powerful potential arguments are distributed across individual WIN entries but never synthesized into unified cross-cutting claims. The review currently makes its case WIN-by-WIN (Part 2), then by falsification test (Part 4), then by internal contradiction (Part 4.5), then by code analysis pattern (Part 4.6). What it lacks is a synthesis layer that shows how these different analytical lenses converge on the same conclusion.

The five major missing arguments (quadruple failure cross-tabulation, axial symmetry paradox, batch registration, duplicate counting reduction, and derives-from-dome devastation) each represent a different way of showing that the dome model's 67 WINs are not 67 independent confirmed predictions. They are approximately 25-30 observations of real phenomena, none of which derive from dome geometry, most of which are already explained by standard physics, presented through a monitoring system that largely validates itself against its own hardcoded values.

The moderate-severity missing arguments (monitor avoidance pattern, kernels-of-truth synthesis, falsifiability asymmetry, eclipse pre-commitment, open problems concession) are more about rhetorical completeness and preempting objections than about new evidence. They would make the review harder to attack without necessarily making the core argument stronger.

Priority for implementation: Arguments #5 (derives-from-dome devastation), #1 (quadruple failure), and #2 (axial symmetry paradox) are the most impactful. They represent genuinely new structural arguments that cannot be dismissed as opinion or framing — they are provable from the dome's own data and equations.

citations_unchecked: This is a holistic review — no specific citations to check
